steps.title: Use the sine addition identity
steps.content: The expression has the exact form $\sin A\cos B+\cos A\sin B$, which equals $\sin(A+B)$. Here, the angles are $24^{\circ}$ and $66^{\circ}$.

steps.title: Apply the sine addition identity
steps.content:
The sine angle-addition identity states:
$$\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$$
Setting $A = 24^\circ$ and $B = 66^\circ$:
$$\sin(24^\circ)\cos(66^\circ) + \cos(24^\circ)\sin(66^\circ) = \sin(24^\circ + 66^\circ) = \sin(90^\circ) = 1$$

steps.title: Use a complementary-angle relationship
steps.content:
Since $24^\circ + 66^\circ = 90^\circ$, the angles are complementary:
$$\cos(66^\circ) = \sin(24^\circ) \quad \text{and} \quad \cos(24^\circ) = \sin(66^\circ)$$
These substitutions will help check which answer choice also equals 1.

steps.title: Check each answer choice against the value 1
steps.content:
• A: $2\left(\cos66^\circ\right)\left(\sin24^\circ\right)$ - Substituting $\cos(66^\circ) = \sin(24^\circ)$ gives $2\sin^2(24^\circ)$. This equals 1 only if $\sin(24^\circ) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, i.e., $24^\circ = 45^\circ$ - false. Eliminated.
  • B: $2\left(\cos66^\circ\right)+2\left(\cos24^\circ\right)$ - This is a sum of cosines multiplied by 2, not a Pythagorean expression. Its value is approximately $2(0.407) + 2(0.914) \approx 2.64 \neq 1$. Eliminated.
  • C: ${\left(\cos66^\circ\right)}^{2}+{\left(\cos24^\circ\right)}^{2}$ - Substituting $\cos(66^\circ) = \sin(24^\circ)$ gives $\sin^2(24^\circ) + \cos^2(24^\circ) = 1$ by the Pythagorean identity. That matches Choice C — ${\left(\cos66^\circ\right)}^{2}+{\left(\cos24^\circ\right)}^{2}$. That confirms C is correct.
  • D: ${\left(\cos66^\circ\right)}^{2}+{\left(\sin24^\circ\right)}^{2}$ - Both terms equal $\sin^2(24^\circ)$ (since $\cos 66^\circ = \sin 24^\circ$), giving $2\sin^2(24^\circ) \approx 2(0.407)^2 \approx 0.33 \neq 1$. Eliminated.
